Snow fall totals in the valley locations ranged between four and eight inches, while the higher terrain received up to a foot of new snow.

June was one of the wettest months recorded in the Pocatello County Warning Area.  Percent of normals at the 39 climate data stations ranged from a low of 198 percent at the Malad Airport to 573 percent at the Blackfoot Fire Department.  6.63 inches of rain fell at Craters of the Moon National Monument.  Record June amounts were recorded at Ketchum Ranger Station with 5.29 inches as well as Burley with 3.07 inches, Idaho Falls with 3.97 inches and the Pocatello Regional Airport with 4.00 inches.  The 4.00 inch total at Pocatello was the wettest month ever recorded at the station.  5.85 inches of rain was recorded at the Blackfoot Fire Station.  The rains caused extensive flooding and flash flooding along with several mud slides over the Castle Rock burn site in Ketchum closing Warm Springs Road often.  A significant flash flood occurred in South Pocatello on June 26th causing extensive damage.  A plus side to the enormous amounts of rainfall is that drought status ended in southeast Idaho and area reservoirs filled to their highest level in years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pocatello Regional Airport recorded 4.00 inches of rainfall for the entire month of June.  This was the wettest June on record as well as the wettest month ever recorded for this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front brought heavy snow to portions of North Idaho.  Behind the warm front, shallow cold air remained in the valleys which resulted in a mixed bag of snow, freezing rain, and freezing drizzle with minor ice accumulations reported.  Icy roadways near Post Falls contributed to a fatal pileup on Interstate 90 involving four vehicles with one indirect fatality and six indirect injuries.  Snow accumulations in the valleys ranged from three to six inches, except for as much as eight inches in Bonners Ferry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt led to flooding in portions of Shoshone, Latah, and Kootenai counties.  The begin and end time of the flooding is estimated.  Near Rose Lake, water was coming up near the levels of homes, with one foundation washed away.  Minor flooding also occurred in Potlach when the Palouse River crested at 15.9 feet, which is 0.9 feet above flood stage.  Several road closures resulted from the flooding in Latah County.  Schools also closed down due to flooding concerns in Potlach, Kendrick, and Julietta.  A couple main stem rivers reached flood stage.  The Coeur D'Alene River at Cataldo crested at 43.1 feet, 0.1 feet above flood stage.  The St. Joe River at Calder crested at an estimated 13-14 feet, up to a foot above flood stage.  Property damage from this event is estimated to be at least $252,000.

Numerous snow showers occurred in the Central Panhandle Mountains, Palouse, and on the Camas Prairie as a cold and unstable weather system tracked through the area.  Upslope flow also enhanced snow showers. For the Central Panhandle Mountains the heaviest snow fell before 5 PM local time, while on the Camas Prairie the heaviest snow fell between 6 PM and 9 PM local time.  Snow accumulations from this event included 12 inches 11 miles northeast of Kendrick, seven inches in Wallace, five inches near Viola, and six inches in Winchester.  The snow led to several accidents on the Palouse.

Numerous snow showers occurred in the Central Panhandle Mountains, Palouse, and on the Camas Prairie as a cold and unstable weather system tracked through the area.  Upslope flow also enhanced snow showers. For the Central Panhandle Mountains the heaviest snow fell before 5 PM local time, while on the Camas Prairie the heaviest snow fell between 6 PM and 9 PM local time.  Snow accumulations from this event included 12 inches 11 miles northeast of Kendrick, seven inches in Wallace, five inches near Viola, and six inches in Winchester.  The snow led to several accidents on the Palouse.

Numerous snow showers occurred in the Central Panhandle Mountains, Palouse, and on the Camas Prairie as a cold and unstable weather system tracked through the area.  Upslope flow also enhanced snow showers. For the Central Panhandle Mountains the heaviest snow fell before 5 PM local time, while on the Camas Prairie the heaviest snow fell between 6 PM and 9 PM local time.  Snow accumulations from this event included 12 inches 11 miles northeast of Kendrick, seven inches in Wallace, five inches near Viola, and six inches in Winchester.  The snow led to several accidents on the Palouse.

A very cold and unstable low pressure system moved through north Idaho with widespread moderate to heavy snow showers in the Coeur D'Alene area, Palouse, and Central Panhandle Mountains.  Several locations picked up between four and seven inches of snowfall.  Locally higher accumulations occurred in St. Maries where a spotter seven miles north of the town picked up a foot of snow; while another spotter five miles west of town picked up eight inches.

A very cold and unstable low pressure system moved through north Idaho with widespread moderate to heavy snow showers in the Coeur D'Alene area, Palouse, and Central Panhandle Mountains.  Several locations picked up between four and seven inches of snowfall.  Locally higher accumulations occurred in St. Maries where a spotter seven miles north of the town picked up a foot of snow; while another spotter five miles west of town picked up eight inches.

An increased pressure gradient due to a passing cold front and unstable conditions allowed strong winds to mix to the surface across portions of north central Idaho. The main areas impacted were the southern Clearwater Mountains and the higher terrain of Lemhi County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The few observing stations in the area recorded wind gusts up to 50 mph.  However, the winds were likely much stronger than this as numerous tree blow downs were reported in Yellowpine and at the Whitewater Ranch, including several trees down on nearby roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increased pressure gradient due to a passing cold front and unstable conditions allowed strong winds to mix to the surface across portions of north central Idaho. The main areas impacted were the southern Clearwater Mountains and the higher terrain of Lemhi County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kriley Creek RAWS reported south winds sustained at 33 mph gusting to 78 mph.  The Salmon RAWS reported south east winds sustained at 19 mph gusting to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system moved into the northern Rockies with abundant moisture. This system was accompanied by a strong cold front, which pushed through the area over the weekend. Initially lower elevations started out with a rain snow mix, then transitioned to snow as the the front moved through the area. Snow fall totals in the valley locations ranged between four and eight inches, while the higher terrain received up to a foot of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Weippe reported seven inches of new snow accumulations.  Pierce reported a storm total snow of five inches.  Higher terrain in the area received up to nine inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter-like storm system moved into the Inland Northwest during the first few days of April.  A very unusual occurrence of heavy, low elevation snow was observed late on April first going into the early morning hours of the second, when a warm front brought widespread snowfall. Snow accumulations in the Moscow area ranged from four to seven inches, with Troy also picking up seven inches.

A strong Pacific low pressure system moved into the northern Rockies with abundant moisture. This system was accompanied by a strong cold front, which pushed through the area over the weekend. Initially lower elevations started out with a rain snow mix, then transitioned to snow as the the front moved through the area. Snow fall totals in the valley locations ranged between four and eight inches, while the higher terrain received up to a foot of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Department of Transportation closed Highway 28 south of Leadore due to snow and blowing snow severely reducing visibility.  Total snow fall amounts ranged from two to four inches in the lower elevations and eight to ten inches over the higher terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous storm system brought snow to portions of north central Idaho. Local gusty west to southwest winds increased as the jet stream pushed through the area, creating areas of reduced visibility in blowing snow. Snow accumulations in the lower elevations ranged from three to five inches, while higher terrain received eight to twelve inches of new snow. Wind gusts between thirty and forty miles per hour were not uncommon with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained storm spotters in Dixie reported eleven inches of new snow.  Storm spotters in Elk City reported anywhere from fourteen to eighteen inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring snowstorm brought heavy snowfall to the mountains of extreme eastern Idaho.  The heaviest amounts ranged from 10 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Bear SNOTEL reported 12 inches of snow.  Grand Targhee ski resort reported 12 inches of snow.  Storm began at noon on March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nine inthes to two feet of snow were common in high elevations and winds with the heavy snow caused road closures on both interstate 15 and interstate 84.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of snow fell at the Mill Creek Summit and Smiley Mountain SNOTEL sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing Pacific storm system impacted north central Idaho with heavy snow fall. Snow fall amounts ranged between five to eight inches over the lower elevations. Numerous car accidents were reported by the DES. With the passage of a cold front, increased winds in the afternoon produced areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The DES in Clearwater County reported seven car accidents due to slick roads in accumulating snow.  The trained spotters in the zone reported about eight inches of new snow accumulations with a report up to fourteen inches twelve miles east of Kooskia.  The power company around this area reported eighty trees down across local roads.  Power and telephone lines were down for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level trough of low pressure brought convective showers with gusty winds and embedded thunderstorms to the northern Rockies. One storm near Orofino, Idaho caused damage to trees and power lines, and backed up traffic on Highway 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe weather spotter reported hearing on a scanner that trees were  blown down onto Highway 12 west of Orofino from gusty winds. A newspaper article confirmed that trees and power lines were blown down onto the highway near the Riverside Motel and Stoddard Electric. Traffic was backed up from the Orofino bridge to the the intersection of 118 Street and Highway 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms dropped penny to quarter-sized hail over portions of Nez Perce and Latah counties.
Severe thunderstorms dropped penny to quarter-sized hail over portions of Nez Perce and Latah counties.
Severe thunderstorms dropped penny to quarter-sized hail over portions of Nez Perce and Latah counties.
Severe thunderstorms dropped penny to quarter-sized hail over portions of Nez Perce and Latah counties.

Persistent heavy rain created river and small stream flooding over portions of Lemhi County in Idaho.  Total precipitation amounts well over an inch were reported.  Lemhi County law enforcement reported up to 12 inches of water over area roadways.  Macfarland Campground was completely sumberged and several houses reported basement flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported three to four inches of water on portions of Highway 28 in areas between Lemhi and Leadore, with up to 12 inches of water flowing over Highway 28 near Lemhi.  Precipitation amounts ranged between one to two inches in the area.  Basement flooding and flooding of Macfarland Campground were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding and dime and nickel sized hail fell as showers and thunderstorms continued to move across parts of Southern Idaho on the 5th of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 5 minutes of nickel and dime sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding and dime and nickel sized hail fell as showers and thunderstorms continued to move across parts of Southern Idaho on the 5th of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads around Bruneau, Idaho were flooded and nearly impassable for almost 2 hours due to about 45 minutes of heavy rain. Law enforcement reported that debris and sediment were cleared from Hot Springs Road after the water had subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm moved into the Pacific Northwest on May 3rd spawning funnel clouds and producing near severe winds and hail up to three quarters of an inch across Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 1.5 miles west of Payette reported one inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread area of slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed over parts of Southern Idaho late June 2nd leading to heavy rainfall amounts and local flash flooding in and around Glenns Ferry, Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of water on several area roads were reported by a local law enforcement officer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked from Priest Lake along Idaho Highway 57 south to just west of Priest River leaving behind a line of damaging winds, knocking down trees and power lines.  Trees as tall as 100 feet were toppled by the winds, with trees falling on Idaho Highway 57.  In Priest River alone in a span of three blocks, ten homes and at least two cars were damaged by downed trees.  Multiple uprooted trees also damaged at least three homes on Kalispell Bay of Priest Lake.  Hail also fell with this storm, with one inch hail reported four miles west-southwest of Coolin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous powerlines were blown down and tree tops were snapped off.  Trees were estimated to be 25-30 feet high and 2-2.5 inches in diameter.  The top of one tree was thrown through the rooftop of an RV.  The metal roof of a home was also lifted in the air.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains during the month of June caused exposed houses in the Hidden Valley subdivision in Pocatello to flood.  As much as 4 inches of water was found in homes exposed to hillsides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains during the month of June caused exposed houses in the Hidden Valley subdivision in Pocatello to flood.  As much as 4 inches of water was found in homes exposed to hillsides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred in Pocatello with large hail reported in southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused by as much a 2 inches of rain in a 2 hour period caused extensive flooding in the south portion of Pocatello.  By 1:15 pm EDT 10th Street and Carter Avenue were completely flooded and not passable.  The Juniper Hills and Riverside golf courses south of the city were completely underwater.  South 5th Avenue was closed due to the water on the street.  US 91 was closed from the South 5th exit to the Portneuf exit.  43 homes and several businesses were reported flooded.  Water up to hoods of cars were reported on Bannock Highway near Riverside Golf Course.   Water submerged cars in the Gables assisted living center on South Grant Avenue.  Roads were closed throughout the Indian Hill section of Pocatello with knee deep water on most roads.  A disaster area declaration was declared for Bannock County at 3:30 pm MDT.  The rains created an 8 foot sink hole at Constitution Park.  Sink holes developed at some local residences as deep as 12 feet.  Extensive road damage occurred due to rushing debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple uprooted trees damaged at least three homes on Kalispell Bay of Priest Lake.

A thunderstorm containing strong outflow winds led to minor damage in Lewiston, with gusts estimated to be as high as 45 mph. Tree limbs were snapped off trees along with a few power outages reported where one utility transformer blew out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Priest Lake Ranger Station reported numerous trees down along Idaho Highway 57.  There was also a loss of power in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms, flash floods and damaging lightning affected parts of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifty eight knot wind gust at the Idaho Falls airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms, flash floods and damaging lightning affected parts of southeast Idaho.
Strong thunderstorms, flash floods and damaging lightning affected parts of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused mudslide on Warm Springs Road at the Castle rock Burn site as reported by the Blaine County Sheriff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms, flash floods and damaging lightning affected parts of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike to a large tree in Taylorsville killed 13 dairy cows who were underneath it for shelter during a thunderstorn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Idaho and the attendant moisture lead to showers and thunderstorms that produced local rainfall amounts of one inch per hour and flash flooding in Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes in Mountain Home, Idaho had water in the basement due to water flowing across the ground from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 1 inch or larger hail in southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broke car wind shield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Idaho and the attendant moisture lead to showers and thunderstorms that produced local rainfall amounts of one inch per hour and flash flooding in Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding was reported by law enforcement along Highway 78 and closed a section from Grandview to Walters Ferry, Idaho. A fatality occured when a vehicle was swept into the Snake River near Murphy, Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry microburst caused wind gusts up to 65 mph with damage near Burley and Paul.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in Burley by a microburst wind with several trees on houses and extensive shingle damage reported.  Light fixtures were shattered on several homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud near Gilmore Summit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud formed from a thunderstorm south of Gilmore Summit and moved towards Mud Creek before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds in excess of 40 mph and gusts in excess of 60 mph affected the Upper and Lower Snake River Plain and Upper Snake River Highlands.  Several roads were closed due to low visibilities in blowing dust and falling trees including interstate 15, Idaho route 33 and US route 20.  Several trees and power lines were felled with power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph and gusts of 55 to 65 mph caused extensive damage and road closures in the region.  Interstate 15 was closed from Idaho Falls to Roberts due to blowing dust and visibilities under 1 mile.  State highway 33 was closed west of Mud Lake from mile post 42 to 44.  A grain elevator in Rexburg was felled by the winds causing $40,000 worth of damage.  Several large trees were felled in Rigby and Zions Bank in Rigby lost their sign.  Several large trees were also felled in Idaho Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds in excess of 40 mph and gusts in excess of 60 mph affected the Upper and Lower Snake River Plain and Upper Snake River Highlands.  Several roads were closed due to low visibilities in blowing dust and falling trees including interstate 15, Idaho route 33 and US route 20.  Several trees and power lines were felled with power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph were measured at Sterling and Blackfoot.  Winds gusted to 55 to 60 mph and an aluminum shed was destroyed in Blackfoot with a 40 foot tree blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds in excess of 40 mph and gusts in excess of 60 mph affected the Upper and Lower Snake River Plain and Upper Snake River Highlands.  Several roads were closed due to low visibilities in blowing dust and falling trees including interstate 15, Idaho route 33 and US route 20.  Several trees and power lines were felled with power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 mph closed US route 20 from Ashton to Ashton Hill.  Power lines were taken down by the winds in Driggs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of thunderstorms containing heavy rainfall impacted Cape Horn Road near Lake Pend Oreille in the Bayview area.  One storm occurred between 3:30 and 4:30 PST, with the second stronger storm between 7:20 and 8:30 PST.  The heavy rain overwhelmed a storm drain and cause some water to flow onto other driveways.  Along Cape Horn Road, a large amount of mud and debris spread across the road making it mostly impassable.

A potent system off the coast of California made it's way inland and tracked through Oregon and Idaho on August 6th, spreading thunderstorms with large hail and strong surface gradient winds into parts of Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Gooding, Idaho reported golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong impulse coming out of an upper low off the coast of California moved through Southwest Idaho with thunderstorms producing gusty winds and periods of heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured gust of 51 MPH was reported, but tree and shingle damage, and extensive blowing dust point to a likely wind in excess of 58 MPH from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent system off the coast of California made it's way inland and tracked through Oregon and Idaho on August 6th, spreading thunderstorms with large hail and strong surface gradient winds into parts of Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported large chunks of hail a little smaller than golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent system off the coast of California made it's way inland and tracked through Oregon and Idaho on August 6th, spreading thunderstorms with large hail and strong surface gradient winds into parts of Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Oreana reported three quarter inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent system off the coast of California made it's way inland and tracked through Oregon and Idaho on August 6th, spreading thunderstorms with large hail and strong surface gradient winds into parts of Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 61 MPH was measured at the Hanson Bridge Idaho Transportation Department site at Twin Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through Southwest Idaho on September 30th bringing post frontal high winds to the Twin Falls and Jerome areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department weather site at Hansen Bridge in Twin Falls recorded a 64 MPH wind gust at 1204 PM and the Jerome ASOS recorded a 59 MPH wind gust at 1216 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm moving into the region from the North Pacific spread heavy snow across most of Southwest Idaho and Southeast Oregon from the fourth through the fifth of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wilson Creek SNOTEL reported 14 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm moving into the region from the North Pacific spread heavy snow across most of Southwest Idaho and Southeast Oregon from the fourth through the fifth of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTELS across Elmore and Boise Counties reported from 7 inches of snow at Bogus Basin to 27 inches at Trinity Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and associated cold front moved into the Northern Rockies, creating a favorable environment for strong hail producing thunderstorms and heavy rain.  Flash flooding was reported outside of Salmon with this event as up to an inch of precipitation occurred in 30 to 45 minutes.  Hail up to the size of golfballs and strong winds nearing 70 mph were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail up to 1.75 inches was reported by the public in Carmen.  In addition, shingles were blown off a roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized flash flooding in Bannock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused road flooding on South Fifth Avenue, Bannock Highway and Samuel and McDougall Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized flash flooding in Bannock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused road flooding on South Fifth Avenue, Bannock Highway and Samuel and McDougall Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph wind gusts measured at Taber.  Power outages reported in the Taber area.  Fifty-eight mph winds at Kettle Butte and 64 mph winds gusts at Sand Dunes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph wind gusts measured at Taber ARL FRD site. Power outages reported in the Taber area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph wind gusts measured at Taber.  Power outages reported in the Taber area.  Fifty-eight mph winds at Kettle Butte and 64 mph winds gusts at Sand Dunes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kettle Butte ARL FRD mesonet site recorded 58 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph wind gusts measured at Taber.  Power outages reported in the Taber area.  Fifty-eight mph winds at Kettle Butte and 64 mph winds gusts at Sand Dunes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty-four mph wind gust at Sand Dunes ARL FRD Mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms on July 4th caused flooding in Inkom and Pocatello and large hail fell also.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over one inch of rain in less than one hour near Inkom and the runoff caused the Rapid Creek and Portneuf River to exceed their banks.  Extensive flooding occurred throughout the city of Inkom.  About 30 homes and businesses flooded with as much as 5 to 12 inches of standing water in them.  Jackson Creek Road was washed out.  Bannock County was declared a disaster area by 3 pm MDT on July 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt led to flooding in portions of Shoshone, Latah, and Kootenai counties.  The begin and end time of the flooding is estimated.  Near Rose Lake, water was coming up near the levels of homes, with one foundation washed away.  Minor flooding also occurred in Potlach when the Palouse River crested at 15.9 feet, which is 0.9 feet above flood stage.  Several road closures resulted from the flooding in Latah County.  Schools also closed down due to flooding concerns in Potlach, Kendrick, and Julietta.  A couple main stem rivers reached flood stage.  The Coeur D'Alene River at Cataldo crested at 43.1 feet, 0.1 feet above flood stage.  The St. Joe River at Calder crested at an estimated 13-14 feet, up to a foot above flood stage.  Property damage from this event is estimated to be at least $252,000.
Strong thunderstorms on July 4th caused flooding in Inkom and Pocatello and large hail fell also.
A strong cold front pushed through the Pacific Northwest on August 29th aiding the development of showers and thunderstorms including large hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Nampa, Idaho reported three quarter inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms, flash floods and damaging lightning affected parts of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KIFI Channel 8 in Idaho Falls reported damage at an Idaho Falls home due to the wind uprooting a pine tree and damaging a newly built fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm resulted in damaging winds in Priest River where at least eight to nine trees were blown down on structures and streets in town.  Roof damage was also noted from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down after a severe thunderstorm passed through Priest River.  Roof damage was also observed from the storm.
